affected_groups: village residents and children in declining-enrollment settlements
content.decision_question: Should national policy default to preventing closure wherever any viable networked configuration exists, or should it accept closure as legitimate once statutory viability thresholds are unmet, provided mitigation is delivered?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can clarify whether networked models are demographically and fiscally viable in specific cases (e.g., whether pooled cohorts across neighboring villages reach minimum thresholds) and whether closure with mitigation reduces measurable harms like commute burden. It cannot resolve whether community preservation should be weighted above administrative efficiency when the two conflict — that is a value choice, not an empirical one.
content.tension: T3's networked retention aims to prevent closure altogether by pooling resources across villages, while T4 accepts closure as an unavoidable 'managed default' for demonstrably non-viable schools and instead focuses on procedural fairness and mitigation. These represent fundamentally different postures toward the same demographic pressure: preservation-at-cost versus dignified consolidation.
content.title: Prevention vs. managed acceptance of closure
